epigraph.text: Enter by the narrow gate... For the gate is narrow and the way is hard that leads to life.

content.text:
When two lines lie in the same plane and never intersect, no matter how 
far they are extended, we call them parallel. This seemingly simple 
concept has profound implications throughout geometry.

content.title: Parallel Lines
content.content:
Two lines are **parallel** if they are coplanar 
and do not intersect. We write $\ell \parallel m$ to indicate that line 
$\ell$ is parallel to line $m$.

content.title: Skew Lines
content.content:
**Skew lines** are lines that are not coplanar. 
They do not intersect and are not parallel.

content.title: Parallel Planes
content.content:
Two planes are **parallel** if they do not 
intersect.

content.title: Transversal
content.content:
A **transversal** is a line that intersects two 
or more coplanar lines at different points.

content.text:
When a transversal crosses two lines, it creates eight angles. These 
angles have special names based on their positions.

content.title: Angle Pairs Formed by a Transversal
content.content:
- **Corresponding angles**: 
 Same position at each intersection ($\angle 1$ and $\angle 5$, 
 $\angle 2$ and $\angle 6$, etc.)

- **Alternate interior angles**: 
 Between the lines, on opposite sides of the transversal 
 ($\angle 3$ and $\angle 6$, $\angle 4$ and $\angle 5$)

- **Alternate exterior angles**: 
 Outside the lines, on opposite sides of the transversal 
 ($\angle 1$ and $\angle 8$, $\angle 2$ and $\angle 7$)

- **Same-side interior angles** 
 (consecutive interior angles): Between the lines, on the same side 
 ($\angle 3$ and $\angle 5$, $\angle 4$ and $\angle 6$)

content.title: Identifying angle pairs
content.problem:
Using the diagram above, identify all pairs of:

1. Corresponding angles

2. Alternate interior angles

marginNotes.text:
What has been is what will be, and 
what has been done is what will be done, and there is nothing new under 
the sun.
